prompt_md: When you statically link a library into your executable, what happens to the library code?

options.a: The entire library file is copied byte-for-byte into the executable
options.b: Only the object files that satisfy unresolved symbols are copied into the executable
options.c: The library remains a separate file and is loaded at runtime
options.d: The library source code is recompiled into your executable

explanation_md: Static linking is selective: the linker walks the archive and copies only the object files whose symbols are actually referenced. `a` is wrong because unused code is left behind. `c` describes dynamic linking. `d` is wrong because linking works on compiled object files, not source.

prompt_md: A program compiled against `libfoo.so` runs on the developer's machine but fails with "cannot open shared object file" on another machine. What is the most likely cause?

options.a: The executable was built with `-Wall` and that flag is not supported everywhere
options.b: The dynamic library `libfoo.so` is not present or not in a searched path on the target machine
options.c: The executable is too large to run on the target machine
options.d: The header file `foo.h` was not installed on the target machine

explanation_md: A dynamic library must be findable at runtime (in system paths, RPATH, or LD_LIBRARY_PATH). The executable itself records only the library name, not its location. Headers and compile flags are irrelevant at runtime.
`add(10, 32)` returns `10 + 32 = 42`. The static library provides the implementation of `add`; the linker copies that definition into the final binary and the call resolves normally.

prompt_md:
On a GNU/Linux system using `ld`, you run:
```
g++ -lmylib main.cpp -o app
```
The build fails with undefined reference errors for symbols from `mylib`. The same command with `-lmylib` moved to the end succeeds. Why?

options.a: Libraries must always be specified before source files on all linkers
options.b: GNU ld resolves symbols left-to-right and discards archive members that satisfy no currently-unresolved symbols
options.c: The `-o` flag must appear before the source file
options.d: GNU ld does not support static libraries listed before `.cpp` files

explanation_md: GNU ld processes the command line left to right. When it sees `-lmylib` before `main.cpp`, no symbols from `main.cpp` are unresolved yet, so it skips the archive. Moving `-lmylib` after `main.cpp` ensures those symbols are already in the unresolved set when the archive is scanned.
